title: Isar (Flutter NoSQL)
objectives: Understand Isar via a real-life analogy
See why it's ~10x faster than SQLite for many workloads

sections.markdown:
**Think of Isar as a race-tuned motorcycle vs SQLite's family sedan.**

- SQLite (with Drift) is a solid, well-understood family sedan. Gets you everywhere, well-known, comfortable, universal.
- **Isar** is a purpose-built race motorcycle from the Hive author. Rebuilt from the ground up in **Rust**, with **memory-mapped I/O**, **zero-copy** deserialization, **compile-time indexed queries**. Its benchmarks show 5-10x throughput of SQLite for typical Flutter workloads.

**Concretely:** Isar is a NoSQL, embedded database for Dart + Flutter. Data is organized into **collections** of typed Dart objects. Queries use a fluent Dart DSL (no SQL). Reactive streams built in. Indexes are compile-time declared. Cross-collection **links** replace SQL foreign keys. Written in Rust for speed + safety.

**Where it shines:**
- **Query-heavy Flutter apps** — 'give me all products where price < 100 and category = electronics'.
- **Offline-first apps with real data** — reactive .watch() queries mean UI auto-updates.
- **High-throughput workloads** — real-time analytics on device, sync engines, chat message stores.
- **Migration from Hive** — same author, same 'Dart-first' feel, way more power.

**Trade-offs:**
- **No SQL** — you can't leverage decades of SQL knowledge.
- **Newer** — smaller community than SQLite; changes over time.
- **Isar 3 vs Isar 4** — Isar 4 was a rewrite; check current stability + docs before adopting.
- **Codegen required** — `build_runner` on every schema change.

**The 2026 reality:** Isar is a strong choice for NEW Flutter apps that want speed + reactive queries + no SQL. Drift/SQLite is the safer, longer-track-record default for the same goals. Both are excellent — the choice often comes down to team preference and whether you want SQL.

**Isar** is a fast, cross-platform, NoSQL database for Flutter and Dart. Written in Rust, with memory-mapped I/O and zero-copy reads. Data lives in **collections** of typed Dart objects (like Hive, but with queries). Supports **rich queries** (fluent DSL), **compile-time indexes**, **cross-collection links** (relations), **reactive streams**, and **full-text search**. From the author of Hive; addresses Hive's query limitations. Best for query-heavy Flutter apps needing high performance without SQL.
## Sample program — a real Isar setup
## Why Isar is fast

rows: Zero garbage-collection overhead; efficient memory management
OS caches pages of the DB in RAM — reads are effectively zero-cost after warm-up
Deserialize objects directly from the mmap'd region; no intermediate buffers
No runtime schema resolution — indexes and layouts baked at build

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Forgetting `writeTxn` for writes.** All writes must go through a transaction. `await isar.writeTxn(() async { await isar.users.put(u); })`.
**Mistake 2: Not indexing common query columns.** Without an index on `email`, `.filter().emailEqualTo(...)` scans all rows.
**Mistake 3: Not calling `.load()` on links.** Links are lazy — `user.orders.length` is 0 until you load.
**Mistake 4: Adopting Isar 4 blindly for a production app.** Isar 4 has been in flux; verify current stability. Isar 3 is well-established.
**Mistake 5: Migration from Hive without a data-copy step.** Hive TypeAdapters and Isar collections are different formats. Migrations need explicit transfer code.
## Best practices
**1. Index every query filter.** `@Index()` on commonly-filtered fields.
**2. Use `writeTxn` for writes, `txn` for reads.** Batching operations in one transaction is much faster.
**3. `IsarLinks` for many-to-many, `IsarLink` for one-to-one.** Bidirectional link management built in.
**4. `.watch(fireImmediately: true)` for reactive UI.** Emits current value + all subsequent changes.
**5. `@Enumerated(EnumType.name)` for enums.** Human-readable, evolvable.
**6. Test on real devices for perf claims.** Benchmarks vary by workload; measure YOUR workload.
## Interview Q&A

**Q1: What is Isar and how is it different from Hive?**

**A:** Isar is a fast, NoSQL, cross-platform database for Flutter + Dart from the Hive author. **Differences from Hive:** (1) **Rich queries** (filter, sort, limit, aggregate) — Hive is KV-only. (2) **Compile-time indexes** — order-of-magnitude faster than Hive iterating. (3) **Cross-collection links** — one-to-many + many-to-many relations without FK ceremony. (4) **Written in Rust with memory-mapped I/O** — 5-10x SQLite throughput for many workloads. **Best for:** query-heavy Flutter apps that want NoSQL + speed + reactive streams. **Not for:** apps that need SQL portability (leverage SQL knowledge, cross-team consistency).

**Q2: How do queries work in Isar?**

**A:** Fluent Dart DSL over compile-time-verified schema. Example:
```dart
isar.users
  .filter()
  .statusEqualTo(UserStatus.active)
  .and()
  .createdAtGreaterThan(cutoff)
  .sortByCreatedAtDesc()
  .limit(20)
  .findAll();
```
Every `.filter*`, `.sortBy*` method is generated from your schema — no strings, no runtime typos. Queries use indexed lookups automatically when available. **`.watch()`** returns a Stream that auto-emits on collection writes. **`findFirst()`, `findAll()`, `count()`, `deleteAll()`** — typical terminal operations. Very fast because Isar's query engine reads directly from memory-mapped pages.

**Q3: What are IsarLinks and IsarLink?**

**A:** Cross-collection relations without SQL foreign keys. **`IsarLink<T>`** — one-to-one (a Order has one User owner). **`IsarLinks<T>`** — one-to-many (a User has many Orders). Declared as `final orders = IsarLinks<Order>();` on the collection class. **Lazy loading:** `user.orders.length` is 0 until `await user.orders.load()`. Bidirectional links can be inverse-populated via `@Backlink(to: '...')`. **Compared to SQL FKs:** no separate join table for many-to-many; Isar handles the mapping. **Compared to embedding:** references maintain identity — updating a User once updates all references.

**Q4: When would you pick Isar over Drift/SQLite?**

**A:** **Pick Isar when:** (1) You don't need SQL (team doesn't require it, workload doesn't need JOINs across many tables). (2) Query speed is critical — benchmarks matter for your app (analytics, sync, real-time). (3) You want a modern, Flutter-first NoSQL. **Pick Drift/SQLite when:** (1) You want SQL knowledge to transfer. (2) You need mature migration + backup ecosystem. (3) You want the safest, longest-track-record choice. **Also:** consider Drift for teams that value cross-platform SQL familiarity (backend engineers use SQL too), Isar for teams that are all Flutter-front-end. **Both are excellent for their niches.**

**Q5: What's the state of Isar 3 vs Isar 4?**

**A:** As of early 2026 — Isar 3 is the stable, widely-used version. Isar 4 has been under long development with significant API changes; adoption depends on current stability. **Recommendation:** for a new production app RIGHT NOW, use Isar 3 unless Isar 4 is documented stable in the docs. Watch the GitHub for status. Historically, Isar has evolved rapidly — this is a trade-off vs SQLite's decades of stability. When Isar 4 stabilizes, migration guides will be provided; for now, prefer Isar 3 for production.

## Real-world usage

- **Growing Flutter apps** that need NoSQL + queries + performance.
- **Offline-first apps with reactive UI**.
- **Analytics on-device**.
- **Chat/message caches** where read/write throughput matters.
- **Migration from Hive** — same 'feel', more power.
- **NOT for** — apps where SQL knowledge should transfer to backend, or apps needing years-established migration tooling.

## Advanced notes

- **`@Enumerated(EnumType.name)`** — store enum as name string (safe against reordering).
- **`@Index(type: IndexType.value)`** — control index storage strategy.
- **Composite indexes** — `@Index(composite: [CompositeIndex('field2')])`.
- **Full-text search** — `@Index(type: IndexType.value, caseSensitive: false)` + `startsWith`, `contains`.
- **Encryption** — via `encryptionKey` when opening Isar.
- **Inspector** — visual DB inspector during dev.
- **Instance sharing across isolates** — via `Isar.getInstance()`.
- **Web support** — via WASM.
- **Backup / export** — file-based; copy the Isar file.
- **Migration between schema versions** — currently manual; watch for improvements.
- **`writeTxnSync` / `txnSync`** — synchronous variants for hot paths.

flashcards.back: Fast NoSQL DB for Flutter; Rust-backed; queries + indexes + streams + links; from Hive author.
Hive = KV only. Isar = KV + queries + indexes + links + streams. Isar is Hive's successor.
IsarLink<T> (1:1) and IsarLinks<T> (1:N). Lazy — call .load() before use.
Rust core + memory-mapped I/O + zero-copy reads + compile-time schema.
Always in isar.writeTxn(() async { ... }). Reads outside a txn are fine.
Isar = NoSQL + speed. Drift = SQL + type safety + reactive. Pick by workload + team.

options: Isar = NoSQL, Drift = SQL. Pick by shape of data + team
